description: All hikers past Scout Lookout must hold a permit. No exceptions, year-round.
cost: $6 per person (lottery application) + $3 per person (if selected)

seasonalWindow.notes: Permits required every day of the year, including winter.

tips: Two lottery windows: seasonal lottery (opens ~3 months ahead, results ~2 months before trip) and day-before lottery (applications accepted 2 days before, results by 6pm the day before).
Seasonal lottery has better odds — apply as early as possible.
Day-before lottery is competitive but a real backup option, especially on weekdays.
Apply for multiple dates to improve your chances.
Permits are non-transferable and checked at Scout Lookout.
Winter permits are easier to win — fewer applicants.

description: Technical canyoneering route through a tube-shaped canyon. Both top-down (canyoneering) and bottom-up (hiking) routes require permits.
cost: $5 per person (lottery application) + $3 per person (if selected)

seasonalWindow.notes: Limited to 80 people per day. Top-down requires canyoneering experience and gear.

tips: Same two-window lottery system as Angels Landing: seasonal (3 months ahead) and day-before.
Bottom-up route is a strenuous hike (9 miles); top-down requires rappelling and route-finding.
Top-down groups should carry canyoneering gear: harness, rappel device, ropes, wetsuit in spring/fall.
Apply early in the seasonal window for best odds.
Weekday permits are significantly easier to win.

description: The full top-down through-hike of The Narrows (16 miles, starting from Chamberlain's Ranch) requires a wilderness permit. The popular bottom-up day hike does NOT require a permit.
cost: $5 per person (lottery application) + $3 per person (if selected)

seasonalWindow.required: when route is open (typically late May – October)
seasonalWindow.notes: Route closed during high water and cold water temps. NPS posts flow rates daily.

tips: Same lottery system: seasonal and day-before windows.
Top-down is a point-to-point route — arrange shuttle to Chamberlain's Ranch trailhead.
Check NPS Virgin River flow rates before committing. Route closes above ~120 cfs.
Dry suits or wetsuits recommended in spring and fall when water is cold.
Bottom-up day hike (from Temple of Sinawava) is free and permit-free — most travelers do this version.

description: All overnight stays outside developed campgrounds require a backcountry permit. This includes backpacking trips on the West Rim Trail, La Verkin Creek, Hop Valley, and other wilderness zones.
where: Zion Wilderness Desk (Visitor Center) or recreation.gov

seasonalWindow.notes: Popular routes (West Rim, La Verkin Creek to Kolob Arch) fill up fast in spring and fall.

tips: Online reservations available up to 2 weeks in advance at recreation.gov.
Walk-up permits sometimes available at the Wilderness Desk, but not guaranteed in peak season.
Designated campsites only — no dispersed camping allowed in Zion.
Bear canisters not required but hang food or use provided poles at backcountry sites.
West Rim Trail (top-down) is the most popular overnight route — book the moment permits open.

description: The largest campground inside the park (190 sites, some riverside). Reservations required — does not accept walk-ups.

seasonalWindow.notes: Open year-round. Electric sites and riverside sites are most popular.

tips: Reservations open 6 months in advance and sell out fast for spring and fall.
Set a calendar reminder for your 6-month booking window.
Riverside sites (loops A and B) are the most desirable — book the second they open.
Electric sites available for RVs and those wanting to charge devices.
Free shuttle stop right at the campground entrance.
